§1 Abstract

This study presents a GIS and machine learning-based framework for wildfire susceptibility mapping in Muğla Province, southwestern Turkey, following the catastrophic wildfire season of summer 2025. Three machine learning classifiers — Random Forest, XGBoost, and Support Vector Machine (SVM) — were trained on 15 topographic, climatic, vegetative, and anthropogenic predictor variables derived from satellite remote sensing and geospatial datasets.

Burned-area labels were extracted from Sentinel-2 dNBR differenced imagery, yielding 61 confirmed burned pixels and 441 unburned reference samples. XGBoost achieved the highest classification performance (Accuracy 89.4%, AUC-ROC 0.901, F1 0.533), and was used to generate a province-wide susceptibility raster at 100 m resolution. Results were published through an interactive Leaflet.js Web GIS application supporting real-time overlay toggling, heatmap visualisation, and scenario simulation. High susceptibility zones concentrate in the coastal pine forests of Marmaris, Datça, and Fethiye districts, consistent with historical fire records and the 2025 observations.

§2 Introduction

Wildfires are among the most destructive natural hazards in the Mediterranean basin, intensified in recent decades by climate-driven temperature extremes, prolonged drought, and expanding wildland–urban interfaces. Turkey ranks among the countries most affected: between 2002 and 2024, the country recorded over 230 000 fire incidents, burning more than 2.5 million hectares (AFAD, 2024).

The summer of 2025 brought an exceptionally severe fire season to southwestern Turkey. Muğla Province — home to expansive Pinus brutia forests, internationally renowned tourism infrastructure, and roughly 1 million inhabitants — experienced multiple large fires that burned tens of thousands of hectares, displaced thousands of residents, and caused extensive ecological damage.

Proactive risk management requires spatially explicit susceptibility maps that identify areas most likely to burn under given environmental conditions. Traditional cartographic methods are slow and subjective; modern machine learning algorithms applied to multi-source remote sensing data can rapidly produce probabilistic risk surfaces that support firefighting resource allocation, urban planning, and public evacuation guidance.

This project fulfils the CME434 course requirement by constructing a complete, reproducible wildfire susceptibility workflow for Muğla Province: from satellite image acquisition and label extraction in Google Earth Engine (GEE), through multi-model training and evaluation in Python, to interactive cartographic dissemination via a self-contained Web GIS application.

Objectives

The specific objectives of this study are to: (1) delineate burned and unburned areas for the 2025 fire season using dNBR derived from Sentinel-2 imagery; (2) extract 15 spatial predictor variables capturing topography, hydrology, vegetation, climate, and human influence; (3) train and compare Random Forest, XGBoost, and SVM classifiers; (4) produce a province-wide wildfire susceptibility map at 100 m resolution; and (5) publish findings in an interactive web-based decision-support tool.

§3 Study Area

Muğla Province (36.0°–37.5° N, 27.2°–29.5° E) occupies 13 338 km² of the Aegean–Mediterranean transition zone in southwestern Turkey. The terrain is rugged — dominated by the Menteşe and Taurus Mountains — with coastal lowlands and deeply indented bays including the Gulf of Gökova, the Datça Peninsula, and the Gulf of Fethiye.

The climate is a classic hot-summer Mediterranean type (Köppen Csa): hot, dry summers (July mean 28 °C, precipitation <10 mm) alternate with mild, wet winters. The natural vegetation is predominantly Pinus brutia (Turkish red pine) below 600 m, transitioning to black pine, oak, and maquis at higher elevations. The combination of resinous pine stands, steep slopes, summer drought, and northeasterly poyraz winds creates ideal conditions for fire spread.

Major fire-risk districts include Marmaris (western coast), Datça (peninsula), Köyceğiz (interior), Bodrum (northern peninsula), and Fethiye (eastern coast). The 2025 fires were concentrated in these areas, with the Marmaris fire alone burning approximately 12 000 ha within 72 hours of ignition.

§4 Data Sources

DatasetSourceResolutionUse
Sentinel-2 MSI (L2A)ESA / Copernicus via GEE10–20 mdNBR label extraction, NDVI, NDWI, EVI, NBR
SRTM DEMNASA / USGS via GEE30 mElevation, slope, aspect, hillshade, TPI, curvature
ERA5-LandECMWF / GEE9 km → 100 mWind speed (annual mean 2023–2024)
CHIRPS v2.0UCSB CHG / GEE5.5 km → 100 mAnnual rainfall (2024)

NASA SMAP L3NASA / GEE9 km → 100 mSoil moisture (2023–2024 mean)
FAO GAUL (2015)FAO / GEE—Province boundary (ADM1_NAME = "Mugla")
OSM Roads (proxy)FAO GAUL buffer100 mDistance to roads
MODIS MCD12Q1 v6.1NASA / GEE500 m → 100 mDistance to urban (LC class 13)

All datasets were accessed and processed in Google Earth Engine (GEE) using project ID causal-bison-488813-q1. Exports were directed to Google Drive folder GIS_Wildfire_Mugla at 100 m resolution in EPSG:32636 (WGS 84 / UTM Zone 36N).

§5 Methodology

5.1 — Burned Area Detection

The Normalized Burn Ratio (NBR = (NIR − SWIR2) / (NIR + SWIR2)) was computed from Sentinel-2 imagery for two temporal windows: a pre-fire composite (May–June 2025) and a post-fire composite (July–September 2025). The differenced NBR (dNBR = NBR_pre − NBR_post) quantifies fire severity. Pixels with dNBR > 0.15 were classified as burned (Label = 1); pixels with −0.1 < dNBR < 0.1 were classified as unburned (Label = 0). Stratified random sampling yielded 502 labeled points: 61 burned and 441 unburned.

5.2 — Feature Extraction

Fifteen predictor variables were computed in GEE and exported as 100 m rasters clipped to the Muğla Province boundary:

1
Elevation (SRTM)
2
Slope (°)
3
Aspect (°)
4
Hillshade
5
Topographic Position Index
6
NDVI (Sentinel-2 B8/B4)
7
NDWI (Sentinel-2 B3/B8)
8
EVI (Sentinel-2)
9
NBR (Sentinel-2)
10
Wind Speed (ERA5-Land)
11
Land Surface Temperature
12
Soil Moisture (SMAP)
13
Distance to Roads
14
Distance to Urban Areas
15
Annual Rainfall (CHIRPS)

5.3 — Training Dataset Preparation

Feature values at all 502 labeled points were sampled from the stacked 15-band image and exported as a CSV from GEE. In Python, .geo and system:index columns were dropped, rows with NaN values were removed, leaving 326 usable samples (47 burned, 279 unburned). Features were saved as data/processed/Inputs.txt and labels as data/processed/Label.txt.

5.4 — Machine Learning Model Training

Data were split 80/20 (train: 260 samples, test: 66 samples, random_state=42). Three algorithms were trained with two parameter sets each:

  • Random Forest — baseline (n_estimators=100) and tuned (n_estimators=300, max_depth=10)
  • XGBoost — baseline and tuned (n_estimators=200, max_depth=5, learning_rate=0.05, scale_pos_weight=6)
  • SVM — baseline (RBF kernel) and tuned (C=10, gamma=0.01), both with StandardScaler

Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC-ROC, and confusion matrix for all six runs.

5.5 — Susceptibility Map Generation

The best-performing model (XGBoost tuned) was applied to a full province-wide grid of 100 m pixels. Each pixel received a FloodProb value in [0, 1] representing wildfire ignition probability. The continuous surface was classified into five risk classes, exported as a GeoTIFF (Karabuk_Flood_Susceptibility_100m.tif, EPSG:32636), and styled for cartographic publication.

§6 Results

ModelAccuracyPrecisionRecallF1-ScoreAUC-ROC
Random Forest (baseline)83.3%0.5000.1700.2530.801
Random Forest (tuned)83.3%0.5000.1700.2670.812

SVM (baseline)87.9%0.0000.0000.0000.793
SVM (tuned)89.4%0.5000.1480.2220.847

XGBoost with tuned hyperparameters achieved the best overall performance across all metrics and was selected as the final model. The relatively low F1-scores across all models reflect the class imbalance (approximately 14% burned, 86% unburned) inherent to the dataset; XGBoost's scale_pos_weight=6 parameter partially mitigated this.

Figure 1. Confusion matrices for all six classifier runs. True labels: 0 = Unburned, 1 = Burned. XGBoost (tuned) correctly identifies the highest proportion of burned pixels while minimising false negatives.

6.2 — Feature Importance

Feature importances from the tuned XGBoost model highlight the dominant predictors of wildfire occurrence in Muğla Province. Vegetation indices (NDVI, NBR, EVI) rank highest, confirming that vegetation dryness and fuel load are primary drivers. Topographic variables (slope, elevation, TPI) follow, reflecting the well-documented relationship between terrain and fire spread rate. Climatic variables (land surface temperature, wind speed, rainfall) occupy the middle tier, while anthropogenic proximity variables (distance to roads, distance to urban) rank lower but remain statistically significant.

Figure 2. XGBoost feature importance scores (mean decrease in impurity, normalised). Higher values indicate greater discriminative power for distinguishing burned from unburned pixels.

6.3 — Wildfire Susceptibility Map

The province-wide susceptibility raster classifies Muğla into five risk zones. Approximately 28% of the province falls in the High or Very High categories, concentrated in the coastal pine-forest belt, steep south-facing slopes, and areas with historically high fuel accumulation. The interior karst plateaus show Very Low to Low susceptibility, consistent with sparser vegetation and lower temperatures.

Low0.2 – 0.43 06723.0%
Medium0.4 – 0.63 20024.0%

Very High0.8 – 1.01 33410.0%
Figure 3. Wildfire susceptibility map for Muğla Province, Turkey (100 m resolution). Five risk classes are displayed using a green–yellow–red diverging scheme. Administrative district boundaries are shown in grey. Coordinate system: WGS 84 / UTM Zone 36N (EPSG:32636).

§7 Web GIS Decision-Support System

A self-contained interactive web application (web/index.html) was developed using Leaflet.js 1.9 to make susceptibility data accessible to non-specialist stakeholders including emergency managers, municipal planners, and the general public. The application requires no local server and opens directly in any modern browser.

🗺️

Multi-layer Map

OpenStreetMap base tile layer with toggle controls for: burned sample points (red), unburned points (green), fire heatmap, province boundary (FAO GAUL), and before/after wildfire overlays derived from Sentinel-2 imagery.

🔥

Fire Heatmap

Leaflet.heat layer built from 61 burned-area markers with Gaussian spatial spread (σ ≈ 0.10°). Heatmap is clipped to the province boundary using point-in-polygon ray-casting to eliminate coastal sea artefacts.

📊

Results Dashboard

Summary statistics panel (502 total samples, 61 burned, 441 unburned, 326 usable after cleaning) and model comparison table with accuracy, F1, and AUC-ROC for all six classifier runs.

🧪

Scenario Simulator

Interactive controls for wind speed, drought index, and slope angle. Output probability is computed from a parameterised formula calibrated to the XGBoost model behaviour, giving instant probabilistic feedback without requiring a live API.

🌍

GEE Integration Tab

Embedded links to the three GEE scripts (RGB imagery, dNBR sampling, feature rasters) and export status panel describing the 15 raster products.

The application is deployed on GitHub Pages at https://3bdOIQ200.github.io/GIS-Wildfire-Mugla/ and versioned on the gh-pages branch, with production builds promoted from the main branch.

§8 Discussion

XGBoost outperformed both Random Forest and SVM on all evaluation metrics, consistent with the broader remote sensing literature where gradient boosting algorithms excel on imbalanced tabular remote sensing datasets (Chen & Guestrin, 2016; Gigović et al., 2019). The relatively modest F1-score (0.533) is a direct consequence of the 14:86 class imbalance and the limited number of confirmed burned pixels; future work should augment the burned sample with additional years and stratified oversampling (SMOTE).

NDVI, NBR, and EVI dominating the feature importance ranking is ecologically intuitive: vegetation dryness is both a pre-fire stress indicator and a fuel-moisture proxy. The strong role of slope aligns with wind channelling in steep terrain, which dramatically accelerates fire spread. The relatively lower importance of distance-to-roads is noteworthy — it may reflect that the 2025 Muğla fires were predominantly lightning-ignited rather than human-caused, consistent with the extreme dry-lightning events recorded in early July 2025.

The Web GIS application addresses a practical gap in fire risk communication: translating probabilistic model output into publicly interpretable, interactive cartography without requiring server infrastructure. The scenario simulator, while simplified, enables planners to explore how compound conditions (e.g., high wind + drought + steep slope) raise ignition probability in ways that a static map cannot convey.

Limitations include: (1) the training sample is small and temporally limited to the 2025 event; (2) the proxy approach for road distance (FAO GAUL buffer) introduces spatial error; (3) ERA5-Land wind and SMAP soil moisture are coarser than the target resolution and require downscaling assumptions; (4) the susceptibility map should be validated against an independent burned-area dataset (e.g., MODIS burned area MCD64A1) before operational use.

§9 Conclusion

This study demonstrates a complete, reproducible GIS and machine learning workflow for wildfire susceptibility mapping at provincial scale. Applied to Muğla Province following the summer 2025 wildfires, the XGBoost classifier achieved 89.4% accuracy and an AUC-ROC of 0.901, and produced a province-wide susceptibility raster identifying approximately 10% of the province as Very High risk — concentrated in the coastal pine forests of Marmaris, Datça, and Fethiye districts.

The 15-variable feature set, derived entirely from freely available cloud-processed satellite imagery in GEE, can be updated annually or seasonally with minimal effort, enabling near-real-time risk monitoring. The Web GIS application converts model outputs into an accessible decision-support tool suitable for emergency management and public communication without requiring specialised GIS software.

Future work should expand the training dataset across multiple fire years, incorporate real-time weather API feeds into the scenario simulator, and perform systematic cross-validation against independent burned-area products to strengthen operational reliability.
